name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/1/3 (set-3)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them.

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION - A
1.

Arrange the following compounds as directed: (any Two)

  1. In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N
  2. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    C6H5NH2, C2H5NH2, NH3
 2
2.

In the plot of molar conductivity {tex}\left(\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} Vs. square root of concentration ({tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}), following curves are obtained for two electrolytes X and Y:

Answer the following:

  1. Predict the nature of electrolyte X and Y.
  2. What happens on extrapolation of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} to concentration approaching zero for electrolytes X and Y?
 2
3.

What do you mean by complex reactions? Can we determine order and molecularity of a complex reaction?

 2

SECTION - B
4.(i)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

 3

OR
(ii)

Define the following terms with a suitable example in each:

  1. Lyophilic sol
  2. Macromolecular colloid
  3. Coagulation
 3
5.(i)

Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.
  2. Carbylamine reaction.
  3. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
 3

OR
(ii)

Write the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow[{0^\circ\mathrm C}]{{\mathrm{HNO}}_2}\mathrm C{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{{\mathrm{NH}}_3}A\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(a\operatorname{) }{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\mathrm C{/tex}
 3
6.

An organic compound X with the molecular formula C5H10O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, does not reduce Tollens' reagent but gives positive iodoform test on heating with I2 in the presence of NaOH. Compound X gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid on vigorous oxidation. Write the

  1. Structure of the compound X.
  2. Structure of the product obtained when compound X reacts with 2,4-DNP reagent.
  3. Structures of the products obtained when compound X is heated with I2 in the presence of NaOH.
 3
7.(i)
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex}.
  2. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN)4]2-.
    (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms:
    Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III)
 3

OR
(ii)

When a co-ordination compound NiCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}6H2O mixed with AgNO3, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write

  1. The structural formula of the complex.
  2. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex.
  3. IUPAC name of the complex.
 3
8.(i)

Account for the following:

  1. Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
  2. Transition metals show variable oxidation states.
  3. Zinc has lowest enthalpy of atomisation.
 3

OR
(ii)

Why transition metals and their compounds act as good catalyst? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples.

 3
9.
  1. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol-1:
    Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol-1)
  2. Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg2+ ion and Cl- ion are 106 S cm2mol-1 and 76.3 S cm2mol-1 respectively.
 3
10.

Following ions of 3d transition series are given:
Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+
(Atomic number : Ti = 22, Fe = 26, Cu = 29, Zn = 30)
Identify the ion, which is

  1. a strong reducing agent in aqueous solution.
  2. more stable than its +1 oxidation state.
  3. colourless in aqueous solution.

Give a suitable reason in each.

 3
11.

A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction. [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 10 = 1]

 3

SECTION - C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids are some of the important classes of organic compounds containing carbonyl group. These are highly polar molecules due to higher electro-negativity of oxygen relative to carbon in the carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by dehydrogenation or controlled oxidation of primary alcohols and controlled reduction of acyl halides. Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.
(i)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.:
CH3COCH3, CH3CHO, HCHO, C6H5COCH3

 1
(ii)

Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between Ethanal and Propanone.

 1
(iii)

Why carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions like aldehydes and ketones?

 1
(iv)
  1. Why {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature?
  2. Write the products in the following:
 2

OR

Write the major products of the following reactions:

 2

Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/1/3 (set-3)

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them.

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Arrange the following compounds as directed: (any Two)

2
  1. In decreasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution:
    C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N
  2. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    C6H5NH2, C2H5NH2, NH3
Solution:
  1. In aqueous solution, basic strength decreases in the order. (C2H5)NH > (C2H5)3N > C2H5NH2
  2. nhIn increasing order of solubility in water:
    (C2H5)NH2> (C2H5)2NH> C6H5NH2
  3. In decreasing order of their pKb values:
    NH3 > C2H5NH2 > C6H5NH2
 2
2.

In the plot of molar conductivity {tex}\left(\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} Vs. square root of concentration ({tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}), following curves are obtained for two electrolytes X and Y:

Answer the following:

  1. Predict the nature of electrolyte X and Y.
  2. What happens on extrapolation of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} to concentration approaching zero for electrolytes X and Y?
Solution:
  1. Electrolyte X is strong electrolyte & Electrolyte Y is weak electrolyte
  2. ^°m for weak electrolytes cannot be obtained by extrapolation while ^°m for strong electrolytes can be obtained as intercept.
 2
3.

What do you mean by complex reactions? Can we determine order and molecularity of a complex reaction?

Solution:

The reactions which occur through a sequence of elementary reactions to give the products.
Yes order of complex reaction can be determined but molecularity has no meaning for complex reaction.

 2

SECTION : B
4.(i)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3

OR
(ii)

Define the following terms with a suitable example in each:

  1. Lyophilic sol
  2. Macromolecular colloid
  3. Coagulation
Solution:

Chapter Deleted from the Textbook.

 3
5.(i)

Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case:

  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.
  2. Carbylamine reaction.
  3. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction.
Solution:
  1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product.
  2. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test.
    {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
    For example,
    {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex}{tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex}+ {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex}
  3. Hoffmann bromamide reaction:
    When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom.

    For example,
 3

OR
(ii)

Write the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow[{0^\circ\mathrm C}]{{\mathrm{HNO}}_2}\mathrm C{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{{\mathrm{NH}}_3}A\xrightarrow[{\text{ (b) }{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(a\operatorname{) }{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}\mathrm B\xrightarrow{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}}\mathrm C{/tex}
Solution:


3
6.

An organic compound X with the molecular formula C5H10O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, does not reduce Tollens' reagent but gives positive iodoform test on heating with I2 in the presence of NaOH. Compound X gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid on vigorous oxidation. Write the

  1. Structure of the compound X.
  2. Structure of the product obtained when compound X reacts with 2,4-DNP reagent.
  3. Structures of the products obtained when compound X is heated with I2 in the presence of NaOH.
Solution:
  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}

  3. CH3-CH2CH2COONa + CHI3
 3
7.(i)
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex}.
  2. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN)4]2-.
    (Atomic number of Ni = 28)
  3. Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms:
    Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III)
Solution:
  1. On the basis of crystal field theory, for a d4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}< P, then the complex is a high spin complex formed by the association of weak field ligands with the metal ion. As a result, the fourth electron enters one of the eg orbitals, thereby, exhibiting the electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1{/tex}.
  2. Ni atom (z = 28)
    E.C of Ni. = [Ar]4s23d8
    E.C. of Ni2+ = [Ar]4s03d8

    It is diamagnetic, due to the absence of unpaired electrons.
    Shape - square planar.
  3. [CoCl2(en)2]
 3

OR
(ii)

When a co-ordination compound NiCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}6H2O mixed with AgNO3, 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write

  1. The structural formula of the complex.
  2. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex.
  3. IUPAC name of the complex.
Solution:
  1. [Ni(H2O)6]Cl2]
  2. Secondary valency = 6
  3. Hexaaquanickel (II) chloride
 3
8.(i)

Account for the following:

  1. Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
  2. Transition metals show variable oxidation states.
  3. Zinc has lowest enthalpy of atomisation.
Solution:
  1. Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
  2. The transition metals have their valence electrons in (n - 1)d and ns orbitals. Since, there is very little energy difference between these orbitals, both energy levels can be used for bond formation. Thus, transition elements exhibit variable oxidation states.
  3. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in Zinc, the interatomic bonding is weak in Zinc. Thus, the enthalpy of atomization of zinc is lowest as the result of this weak metallic bonding.
 3

OR
(ii)

Why transition metals and their compounds act as good catalyst? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples.

Solution:

In case of transition elements, the variability in the oxidation state is due to the participation of (n-1)d orbitals and ns orbitals.
The oxidation states differ by unity. Thus, V shows +2,+3,+4 and +5 oxidation states and Mn shows +2,+3,+4,+5,+6 and +7 oxidation states.
On the other hand, the variable oxidation states shown by some p-block elements differ by two units. Thus, Sn shows +2 and +4 oxidation state and indium shows +1 and +3 oxidation states.

 3
9.
  1. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol-1:
    Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol-1)
  2. Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg2+ ion and Cl- ion are 106 S cm2mol-1 and 76.3 S cm2mol-1 respectively.
Solution:
  1. Zn(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s)
    The number of moles of electrons transferred (n) is 2, as two moles of electrons are involved in the reduction of two moles of {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{\wedge}{/tex} + ions.
    Now, you are given {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300{tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex}, but you need to convert it to joules (J) by multiplying by 1000 (since 1 kJ = 1000 J):
    {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300{tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex}1000 {tex}\frac{J}{kJ}{/tex} = -300,000{tex}\frac{J}{mol}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}{/tex}
    Now, plug in the values into the equation and solve for Eo:
    -300,000 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex}= -(2 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~C}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} Eo
    Given the cell reaction:
    Let's calculate Eo:
    Eo = {tex}\frac{-300,000 \frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}}{-(2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 96500 \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{mol}})}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.11 V
    So, the standard cell potential Eo for the given cell reaction is approximately 3.11 V.
  2. MgCl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg2+ + 2Cl-
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right){/tex} + {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = 106S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 76.3S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} = 258.6S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex}
    The limiting molar conductivity of MgCl2 is 258.6 S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex}
 3
10.

Following ions of 3d transition series are given:
Ti2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+
(Atomic number : Ti = 22, Fe = 26, Cu = 29, Zn = 30)
Identify the ion, which is

  1. a strong reducing agent in aqueous solution.
  2. more stable than its +1 oxidation state.
  3. colourless in aqueous solution.

Give a suitable reason in each.

Solution:
  1. Fe2+, Fe is more stable in Fe3+ due to half-filled d5 configuration.
  2. Cu2+, due to high {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} H^{\circ}{/tex} of Cu2+ than Cu+.
  3. Zn2+, no unpaired electrons / no d-d transition.
 3
11.

A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction. [Given: log 2 = 0.3010, log 10 = 1]

Solution:

{tex}\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{40 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}}{/tex}
90% completion
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac {\left[\mathrm{R}_0\right]} {[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303} { 0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 {tex}\times{/tex} log{tex}\frac{100}{10}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 132.9 min

 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids are some of the important classes of organic compounds containing carbonyl group. These are highly polar molecules due to higher electro-negativity of oxygen relative to carbon in the carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by dehydrogenation or controlled oxidation of primary alcohols and controlled reduction of acyl halides. Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.

 5
(i)

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.:
CH3COCH3, CH3CHO, HCHO, C6H5COCH3

Solution:

C6H5COCH3 < CH3COCH3 < CH3CHO < HCHO

 1
(ii)

Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between Ethanal and Propanone.

Solution:

On heating with tollens' reagent, ethanal forms silver mirror whereas propanone does not.

 1
(iii)

Why carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions like aldehydes and ketones?

Solution:

Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.

 1
(iv)
  1. Why {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature?
  2. Write the products in the following:
Solution:
  1. Due to strong electron withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilization of the conjugate base.

 2

Solution:

Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed. (C 2 H 5 )NH > (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N > C 2 H 5 NH 2 nh In increasing order of solubility in water: (C 2 H 5 )NH 2 > (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH> C 6 H 5 NH 2 In decreasing order of their pKb values: NH 3 > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 H 5 NH 2 2 2. What happens on extrapolation of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} to concentration approaching zero for electrolytes X and Y? Solution: Electrolyte X is strong electrolyte & Electrolyte Y is weak electrolyte ^°m for weak electrolytes cannot be obtained by extrapolation while ^°m for strong electrolytes can be obtained as intercept. 2 3. What do you mean by complex reactions? Can we determine order and molecularity of a complex reaction? Solution: The reactions which occur through a sequence of elementary reactions to give the products. Yes order of complex reaction can be determined but molecularity has no meaning for complex reaction. 2 SECTION : B 4. (i) Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption. Solution: Chapter Delete from the Textbook. 3 OR (ii) Define the following terms with a suitable example in each: Lyophilic sol Macromolecular colloid Coagulation Solution: Chapter Deleted from the Textbook. 3 5. (i) Illustrate the following reactions giving suitable example in each case: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. Carbylamine reaction. Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. Solution: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: is used for the preparation of aliphatic primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is first of all treated with ethanolic KOH to form potassium phthalimide. Potassium phthalimide on treatment with alkyl halide gives N-alkyl phthalimide, which on hydrolysis with dilute hydrochloric acid gives a primary amine as the product. Carbylamine reaction: is used as a test for the identification of primary amines. When aliphatic and aromatic primary amines are heated with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide, carbylamines (or isocyanides) are formed. These carbylamines have very unpleasant odours. Secondary and tertiary amines do not respond to this test. {tex}\underset{Primary\;amine}{R-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{Carbylamine}{R-NC}{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} For example, {tex}\underset{Methnamine}{CH_3-NH_2}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{Chloroform}{CHCL_3}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;\underset{hydroxide}{\underset{Potassium}{3KOH(alc.)}}\;{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow\triangle\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{or\;methyl\;isocyanide}{\underset{Methyl\;Carbylamine}{CH_3-NC}}\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3KCL\;{/tex} + {tex}\;3H_2O{/tex} Hoffmann bromamide reaction: When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide is produced. This degradation reaction is known as Hoffmann bromamide reaction. This reaction involves the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon atom of the amide to the nitrogen atom. (i) On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration for d 4 ion if {tex}\Delta_0<P{/tex} . Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- . (Atomic number of Ni = 28) Write the formula of the following complex using IUPAC norms: Dichloridobis (ethane-1,2-diamine) cobalt (III) Solution: On the basis of crystal field theory, for a d 4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} < P, then the complex is a high spin complex formed by the association of weak field ligands with the metal ion. As a result, the fourth electron enters one of the e g orbitals, thereby, exhibiting the electronic configuration {tex}t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1{/tex} . Ni atom (z = 28) E.C of Ni. = [Ar]4s 2 3d 8 E.C. of Ni 2+ = [Ar]4s 0 3d 8 It is diamagnetic, due to the absence of unpaired electrons. Shape - square planar. [CoCl 2 (en) 2 ] 3 OR (ii) When a co-ordination compound NiCl 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 6H 2 O mixed with AgNO 3 , 2 moles of AgCl are precipitated per mole of the compound. Write The structural formula of the complex. Secondary valency of Ni in the complex. IUPAC name of the complex. Solution: [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 2 ] Secondary valency = 6 Hexaaquanickel (II) chloride 3 8. (i) Account for the following: Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. Transition metals show variable oxidation states. Zinc has lowest enthalpy of atomisation. Solution: Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. The transition metals have their valence electrons in (n - 1)d and ns orbitals. Since, there is very little energy difference between these orbitals, both energy levels can be used for bond formation. Thus, transition elements exhibit variable oxidation states. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in Zinc, the interatomic bonding is weak in Zinc. Thus, the enthalpy of atomization of zinc is lowest as the result of this weak metallic bonding. 3 OR (ii) Why transition metals and their compounds act as good catalyst? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples. Solution: In case of transition elements, the variability in the oxidation state is due to the participation of (n-1)d orbitals and ns orbitals. The oxidation states differ by unity. Thus, V shows +2,+3,+4 and +5 oxidation states and Mn shows +2,+3,+4,+5,+6 and +7 oxidation states. On the other hand, the variable oxidation states shown by some p-block elements differ by two units. Thus, Sn shows +2 and +4 oxidation state and indium shows +1 and +3 oxidation states. 3 9. The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol -1 : Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol -1 ) Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl 2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg 2+ ion and Cl - ion are 106 S cm 2 mol -1 and 76.3 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively. Solution: Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) The number of moles of electrons transferred (n) is 2, as two moles of electrons are involved in the reduction of two moles of {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{\wedge}{/tex} + ions. Now, you are given {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300 {tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} , but you need to convert it to joules (J) by multiplying by 1000 (since 1 kJ = 1000 J): {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -300 {tex}\frac{kJ}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} 1000 {tex}\frac{J}{kJ}{/tex} = -300,000 {tex}\frac{J}{mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}{/tex} Now, plug in the values into the equation and solve for E o : -300,000 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex} = -(2 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{~C}}{\mathrm{mol}}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} E o Given the cell reaction: Let's calculate E o : E o = {tex}\frac{-300,000 \frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{mol}}}{-(2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 96500 \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{mol}})}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.11 V So, the standard cell potential E o for the given cell reaction is approximately 3.11 V. MgCl 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg 2+ + 2Cl - {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right){/tex} + {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right){/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2\right){/tex} = 106S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 76.3S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} = 258.6S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} The limiting molar conductivity of MgCl 2 is 258.6 S {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{cm^2}{mol}{/tex} 3 10. Following ions of 3d transition series are given: Ti 2+ , Fe 2+ , Cu 2+ , Zn 2+ (Atomic number : Ti = 22, Fe = 26, Cu = 29, Zn = 30) Identify the ion, which is a strong reducing agent in aqueous solution. more stable than its +1 oxidation state. colourless in aqueous solution. Give a suitable reason in each. Solution: Fe 2+ , Fe is more stable in Fe 3+ due to half-filled d 5 configuration. Cu 2+ , due to high {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} H^{\circ}{/tex} of Cu 2+ than Cu + . Zn 2+ , no unpaired electrons / no d-d transition. 3 11. A first order reaction is 50% complete in 40 minutes. Calculate the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
